acceptance_targets: [F14-02] "Particular" and "specific" as adjectives modifying abstract or experiential nouns appear no more than 4 times combined in the revised chapter. Each surviving instance modifies a noun that genuinely benefits from the qualifier (i.e., the adjective disambiguates rather than merely intensifies).
[F14-03] "The way" as a comparative or habitual-description connective appears no more than 4 times in the revised chapter. No sentence contains more than one instance.

[F14-04] The "the [abstraction] of a [person/type] who [clause]" construction appears no more than 3 times in the revised chapter. At least 2 of the eliminated instances are replaced with constructions that show the quality through action or sensory detail rather than naming it.
[F14-05] The "not X, not Y, [different thing]" negation pattern appears no more than twice in the revised chapter. No two surviving instances appear within 30 lines of each other.

[F14-07] The closing paragraph contains at least two concrete, observed sensory details from the Brooklyn environment. It does not use the words "decide," "commit," or "between" (or direct synonyms) to describe the weather or season. The reader can feel the emotional resonance between Nora's state and the external world without the narration making the connection explicit.

findings.acceptance_test: "Particular" and "specific" as adjectives modifying abstract or experiential nouns appear no more than 4 times combined in the revised chapter. Each surviving instance modifies a noun that genuinely benefits from the qualifier (i.e., the adjective disambiguates rather than merely intensifies).
findings.evidence: snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_14.md — "particular" as intensifying adjective appears 5 times: line 29 ("a particular signal"), line 41 ("the particular discomfort"), line 59 ("the particular way"), line 143 ("the particular quality"), line 193 ("the particular quiet"). "Specific" as intensifying adjective appears 4 times: line 85 ("the specific architecture," "her specific daughter"), line 105 ("the specific dread"), line 179 ("the specific relief"), line 227 ("the specific vertigo"). Total: 9 instances of the same specifying-intensifier pattern.
findings.problem: The chapter relies on "particular" and "specific" as modifiers to claim precision for abstract nouns rather than earning precision through concrete detail. Nine instances of effectively the same move — placing an intensifier before an abstraction to make it feel observed — creates a flattening effect. These words promise specificity without delivering it; the actual specificity comes from the surrounding context, not the adjective.
findings.rewrite_direction: Cut at least 5 of the 9 instances across snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_14.md:29, :41, :59, :85, :105, :143, :179, :193, :227. In most cases, deleting the adjective strengthens the noun phrase ("the discomfort of being overheated" is stronger than "the particular discomfort of being overheated"). In cases where specificity is genuinely needed, replace the adjective with concrete detail that earns the claim. Retain no more than 2 instances of "particular" and 2 of "specific" in the full chapter.

findings.acceptance_test: "The way" as a comparative or habitual-description connective appears no more than 4 times in the revised chapter. No sentence contains more than one instance.
findings.evidence: snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_14.md — "the way" as comparative/descriptive connective appears 9+ times: line 5 ("the way he arrived at everything"), line 59 ("the particular way you thought about"), line 75 ("the way she would present a manuscript"), line 76 ("the way a retired English teacher heard syntax"), line 155 ("the way she'd been telling Simone things"), line 185 ("the way a well-placed concession in a manuscript"), line 187 ("the way it always did when work was not fine"), line 199 ("the way she'd been checking for three days, the way you checked on something" — two instances in a single sentence).
findings.problem: "The way" becomes the chapter's dominant rhythmic connective for drawing comparisons, establishing habitual behavior, and introducing simile. At 9+ occurrences it creates a monotonous cadence across otherwise varied passages. Two instances in a single sentence (line 199) make the pattern audible.
findings.rewrite_direction: Reduce to no more than 4 instances across the chapter. Target the least essential uses for elimination: snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_14.md:59 (cut "particular way" entirely), :185 (cut the manuscript analogy — see F14-06), :187 (rephrase), :199 (the double occurrence can be compressed to a single construction). Replace with direct description, embedded clauses, or compression. Do not simply substitute "the manner in which" — vary the underlying sentence architecture.

findings.acceptance_test: The "the [abstraction] of a [person/type] who [clause]" construction appears no more than 3 times in the revised chapter. At least 2 of the eliminated instances are replaced with constructions that show the quality through action or sensory detail rather than naming it.
findings.evidence: snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_14.md — The construction "the [abstract noun] of a [person/situation] who [clause]" appears 6 times: line 7 ("the attention of a woman who read for a living"), line 49 ("the restraint of a woman who'd learned through her own divorce"), line 85 ("the fear of a mother who had been watching her specific daughter"), line 105 ("the specific dread of someone looking for evidence" / "the ordinary curiosity of someone googling a new romantic interest"), line 179 ("the specific relief of hearing her own best instincts echoed back"), line 193 ("the particular quiet of a building where everyone was either out or asleep").
findings.problem: This genitive-abstraction construction is the chapter's default method for characterizing emotional states and secondary figures. It names the quality, then defines the type of person who produces it. At 6 instances, it creates a processional rhythm — each emotional beat arrives in the same syntactic frame. The construction keeps the reader at one remove from the experience by abstracting it into a category before the reader encounters it directly.
findings.rewrite_direction: Retain no more than 3 instances across the chapter. The strongest candidates for retention are snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_14.md:7 (establishes Nora's editorial consciousness) and :85 (Diane's maternal specificity). For the others, show the quality through action, physical detail, or direct interiority rather than naming it through an abstracting genitive. At line 49, for example, let Margo's restraint be visible in what she does next rather than labeled as a quality of a type of person.

findings.acceptance_test: The "not X, not Y, [different thing]" negation pattern appears no more than twice in the revised chapter. No two surviving instances appear within 30 lines of each other.
findings.evidence: snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_14.md — The negation-triad pattern ("not X, not Y, [something else]") appears 4 times: line 31 ("not anger, not hurt, something closer to the flinch"), line 201 ("not willing to call love in case it turned out to be something else and not willing to call anything else in case it turned out to be love"), line 209 ("not a period, not a question mark, something in between that grammar didn't have a name for"), line 229 ("not knowing, and going on anyway, and the going on not feeling like courage or foolishness but like the only direction left").
findings.problem: The negation-triad is the chapter's default syntactic strategy for expressing Nora's ambivalence — she defines her emotional state by naming what it is not, then gesturing toward what it might be. Each instance is individually effective, but 4 occurrences in a single chapter turn the construction into a visible template. The final two instances (lines 209, 229) are within 20 lines of each other, making the repetition audible in the chapter's climactic passage.
findings.rewrite_direction: Retain the 2 strongest instances. The punctuation metaphor at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_14.md:209 is the chapter's most original use; line 31 (the flinch) is vivid and well-placed. For lines 201 and 229, rebuild the expression of ambivalence using different syntactic strategies — a direct contradictory pair, an image that holds both states, or a physical gesture that embodies the unresolved tension. Do not cluster surviving instances within 30 lines of each other.

findings.acceptance_test: The closing paragraph contains at least two concrete, observed sensory details from the Brooklyn environment. It does not use the words "decide," "commit," or "between" (or direct synonyms) to describe the weather or season. The reader can feel the emotional resonance between Nora's state and the external world without the narration making the connection explicit.
findings.evidence: snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_14.md:231 — "Outside, March did what March does in Brooklyn — couldn't decide, wouldn't commit, hung between what it had been and what it was becoming, the last ice on the sidewalks going grey and soft in the gutters, the season not yet arrived but no longer deniable."
findings.problem: The closing pathetic fallacy mirrors Nora's emotional state so directly that it reads as thesis statement rather than image. "Couldn't decide, wouldn't commit" explicitly restates Nora's ambivalence from the preceding paragraphs. "Hung between what it had been and what it was becoming" summarizes the chapter's emotional arc. The preceding paragraph (lines 228-230) has already established Nora's state of suspended irresolution; the weather close does not deepen it, it repackages it. A shortlist-level close would let the external world provide counterpoint or resonance, not annotation.
findings.rewrite_direction: Revise snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_14.md:231 to let the March image carry genuine weather-observation rather than allegorical transparency. The image should rhyme with Nora's state obliquely — through sensory detail, texture, or physical presence — rather than restating the emotional vocabulary of the chapter ("decide," "commit," "between"). Consider ending on a concrete physical detail from the cityscape that the reader connects to Nora's state without being told to. The melting ice is a good image; let it do the work without the preamble of "couldn't decide, wouldn't commit."

non_negotiables: Maintain heading contract exactly (# Chapter N).
Elliptical clipping is not permitted in dialogue or narration.
Avoid transcript cadence in high-stakes dialogue.
Avoid stiff over-formality in dialogue; use contractions and colloquial rhythm where character-true.
Reduce procedural drift when it displaces embodied consequence.
Maintain character-specific voice signatures from style bible.
Honor aesthetic risk policy: sanitization is the primary craft risk — do not soften, euphemize, or retreat from the story's established register. When in doubt, err toward rawness.

revision_pass.focus: Focus on prose and copyedit finish: diction precision, rhythm control, sentence-level clarity, grammar, line-level polish without flattening style, and explicit aesthetic-risk audit (sanitization is the primary risk — do not soften or euphemize; push toward creative risk and specificity).
revision_pass.label: prose/copyedit fixes

aesthetic_risk_policy.creative_risk_policy: This novel must commit fully to its dark comedy register. The humor should be genuinely funny — not arch, not safe, not 'literary funny' where the reader smiles wryly. The darkness should be genuinely dark — the financial manipulation is theft, the emotional manipulation is a form of violence, and the sexual dimension of the AI relationship (intimate photos sent to a non-person, sexual conversations with an algorithm controlled by your best friend) is deeply violating. The novel should not flinch from the specific perversity of Simone reading Nora's intimate messages, of Simone helping Nora dress up for a man Simone invented, of Simone comforting Nora about a fight Simone scripted. These moments are the novel's payload. If they are rendered obliquely or summarized, the book fails. Equally, the genuine tenderness of the friendship — the years of real love underneath the betrayal — must be rendered with the same commitment. The reader should feel both the comedy and the horror simultaneously, not in alternation.
aesthetic_risk_policy.euphemism_penalty: Euphemism is treated as a craft failure equivalent to a plot hole. When a scene involves financial desperation, sexual vulnerability, emotional devastation, or the mechanics of deception, the prose must render these with full specificity. 'She felt used' is never acceptable when you can show what being used feels and looks like in the body and in the specific details of the moment. The reader should not be protected from the characters' experiences.

dialogue_rules.default_contraction_use: High — contractions are the norm. Uncontracted forms reserved for emphasis, deliberate formality, or character-specific register choices. If a line reads stiffly, the first fix is to contract.

prose_style_profile.chapter_texture_variance: Alternate between hot and quiet chapters. After a high-intensity scene (confrontation, discovery, sexual encounter), the next chapter should breathe — slow down, change the sensory environment, let the character do something ordinary. Vary scene counts: some chapters are a single sustained scene, others are three or four. Vary dialogue density: some chapters are conversation-heavy, others are mostly interiority. No two consecutive chapters should open the same way — if one opens in a location, the next should open in thought or in mid-conversation. The novel's middle section (chapters 8-13) should have more structural variety than the opening, which can afford to be more regular.
prose_style_profile.diction: Contemporary, educated, specific. No genre-thriller diction. No prestige-novel stiffness. The language should feel like how smart, funny, self-aware women in their mid-thirties actually think and speak. Cultural references are allowed when organic. Brand names when they carry social information. The prose should be able to be funny without stepping outside the character's perspective to wink at the reader.
prose_style_profile.narrative_distance: Close third person — very close, almost inhabiting the POV character's thought patterns and verbal rhythms. The prose should feel like the character's consciousness rendered in third person, not an external narrator describing the character. When in Nora's sections, the prose has her warmth and her specific noticing. When in Simone's sections, the prose has her edge and her accounting.
prose_style_profile.rhythm_target: Variable. Default to mid-length sentences with natural cadence. Use compression for emotional peaks and psychological precision. Use longer, more digressive rhythms for scenes of intimacy, humor, and daily texture. Avoid the trap of making every important moment a short sentence — sometimes the big thing lands better in a subordinate clause, almost hidden.
